title: The Hidden Years
subtitle: The Lord Jesus from His twelfth year to the threshold of His thirtieth — the long silence of God among men

stillness: Attend well here, and be still.
He withdrew Himself from the company of men, and shunned their tumult.
Enter now that long silence with Him.
gospel: And He went down with them,
And there He dwelt with them
until the beginning of His thirtieth year.
Nor is it found in the Scriptures
that in all this time He did anything —
behold: See how He who is the supreme Master
began first to do, and only after to teach.
He withdrew Himself from the company of men,
and shunned their tumult,
and was constant in frequent prayers.
He went to the synagogue — that is, to the church —
and stood there long in prayer,
setting Himself in the lowlier and more humble place.
He returned home, and stayed with His Mother
and with His foster-father,
and at times He helped them.
Going and returning, He passed among men
as though He found no men there at all.
All marveled, seeing a Youth so beautiful
do nothing, as far as it appeared, worthy of praise.
They looked for Him to do magnificent things,
the works of upright men;
but behold, growing and coming to His twentieth year,
and even to His twenty-fifth and beyond,
He did no works that showed any mark of excellence.
They were utterly amazed at Him, and mocked Him,
and among all alike He was reckoned vile and abject.
consider: Mark well: by doing nothing, He did great things.
For nothing of His deeds is empty of mystery;
as He spoke and worked with power,
so with power He kept silence and withdrew Himself.
This Master, who would one day teach the virtues
and the way of life, began from His youth
to do works of virtue — but in a manner
strange and unknown, unheard of in former ages:
making Himself, in men's sight, useless,
abject, and foolish.
Rightly was it said of Him through the Prophet:
I am a worm and no man,
the reproach of men and the outcast of the people.
All they that saw me have laughed me to scorn;
they have spoken with the lips, and shaken the head.
So He made Himself vile and abject to all.
Does this seem a small thing to you?
In all our works none is reckoned greater or harder.
He has reached the highest and most difficult step
who comes to this: that truly, and not in pretense,
he so conquers and masters his own soul,
and the proud arrogance of his flesh,
that he refuses to be thought of any account,
but is content to be despised as abject and vile.
It is utterly abominable if a little worm exalts itself —
a worm, and food for worms hereafter —
there where the Lord of majesty humbled and abased Himself.
If, then, you ask why the Lord lived thus,
I answer: not because He had need,
but to instruct us.
And so, if we do not learn, we are without excuse.
prayer: O Jesus, meek, the very pattern of true patience,
drive far from me all swelling of pride,
and every appetite of vain glory,
every kind at once of so great a plague
Let there not be, O Lord, nor even seem to be,
any sign of so great an evil and so great a ruin
not in my conduct, not in my words,
nor before You in my thoughts.
Found me in true and deep humility,
that no place may lie open in me
to the snares of my enemies,
and grant that I may be little in my own eyes,
that I may find full grace
in the eyes of Your majesty. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Ego sum vermis et non homo, opprobrium hominum et abjectio plebis.
latinJewel.english: I am a worm and no man, the reproach of men and the outcast of the people.
